title: Antenna Connectors
note: Practical Wi-Fi antenna-connector reference: enterprise APs, outdoor bridges, embedded radios, and WLAN test gear. Almost every connector here is 50 ohm; the F-Type (75 ohm CATV) is the lone exception. RP (reverse-polarity) variants exist on Wi-Fi gear to satisfy FCC rules that discourage swapping in higher-gain antennas. Key field gotcha: an RP connector will thread onto its standard counterpart but the center contacts will not connect. 2026-06-06: RF Connectors merged into this single Antenna Connectors reference.

connectors.reverse_polarity: Yes (this is the RP variant of TNC)
connectors.typical_wifi_use: Enterprise APs with external antennas (Cisco, Aruba, and others)
connectors.mating: Mates only with RP-TNC. Threads onto a standard TNC but center contacts will not connect.
connectors.notes: One of the most common enterprise Wi-Fi connectors. The RP design satisfies FCC antenna-swap rules.

connectors.typical_wifi_use: RF systems and some wireless bridges; rare on Wi-Fi gear vs RP-TNC
connectors.impedance: 50 ohm (75 ohm series also exists; not used for Wi-Fi)
connectors.mating: Mates only with standard TNC. Will not connect with RP-TNC.
connectors.notes: Threaded microwave version of the bayonet BNC. Designed by Neill (Bell Labs) and Concelman (Amphenol).

connectors.reverse_polarity: Yes (this is the RP variant of SMA)
connectors.typical_wifi_use: Consumer Wi-Fi routers, WLAN adapters, many enterprise external antennas
connectors.frequency: Up to 18 GHz (well past 6 GHz / Wi-Fi 6E/7)
connectors.mating: Mates only with RP-SMA. Will physically thread onto a standard SMA, but the center pin/socket are reversed so it will not connect electrically.
connectors.notes: The most common connector on consumer Wi-Fi gear. RP design is an FCC-driven antenna-swap deterrent.

connectors.typical_wifi_use: RF test gear, antennas, SDRs; Wi-Fi gear usually uses RP-SMA instead
connectors.frequency: DC to 18 GHz (some variants to 26.5 GHz)
connectors.mating: Mates only with standard SMA. Will not connect with RP-SMA.
connectors.notes: Standard RF connector. On Wi-Fi product, an SMA where you expected RP-SMA is a giveaway of test/lab or non-consumer hardware.

connectors.full_name: Type-N (invented 1940s by Paul Neill, Bell Labs)
connectors.typical_wifi_use: Outdoor APs, point-to-point bridges, surge arrestors, outdoor antennas
connectors.impedance: 50 ohm (75 ohm version exists for cable TV; not used for Wi-Fi)
connectors.frequency: Up to ~11 GHz standard (precision N to 18 GHz)
connectors.mating: Mates only with standard N. RP-N is a separate, incompatible variant.
connectors.notes: Rugged, weather resistant, low loss. The default for outdoor Wi-Fi and bridges.

connectors.reverse_polarity: Yes (this is the RP variant of N)
connectors.typical_wifi_use: Specialized Wi-Fi deployments; appears in the Cisco DART ecosystem (RP-N-to-DART adapter cables)
connectors.mating: Mates only with RP-N. Will not connect with standard N.
connectors.notes: Uncommon overall, but not vanishingly rare in the Cisco external-antenna world.

connectors.typical_wifi_use: Some enterprise and industrial WLAN equipment
connectors.frequency: Return-loss optimized to ~6 GHz; usable to 18 GHz
connectors.notes: SMA-derived, electrically SMA-like, but swaps the screw thread for a fast push-pull. Created by the Quick Lock Formula Alliance (2003). Faster to mate in dense installs.

connectors.typical_wifi_use: Industrial RF systems; less common in Wi-Fi
connectors.notes: The N-class sibling of QMA from the same Quick Lock Formula Alliance. Quick-mate alternative to threaded N.

connectors.typical_wifi_use: Internal AP antennas, IoT radios, embedded modules
connectors.mating: Mates only with MMCX. Smaller than MCX; the two do not interchange.
connectors.notes: Very small, common inside devices. The smaller of the MCX/MMCX pair.

connectors.mating: Mates only with MCX. Larger than MMCX; the two do not interchange.
connectors.notes: Larger than MMCX, same snap-on idea.

connectors.full_name: Hirose U.FL (ultra-miniature coax; also AMC, UMCC)
connectors.typical_wifi_use: Internal Wi-Fi modules, M.2 cards, AP daughterboards
connectors.mating: Intermateable with I-PEX MHF I (MHF1), AMC and UMCC (same 3.0x3.1 mm footprint, 2.0 mm OD). NOT compatible with the smaller MHF 4.
connectors.notes: Extremely common inside laptops and APs. Rated for limited mating cycles; intended as a near-permanent internal connection. Hirose rates U.FL to ~6 GHz; the intermateable I-PEX MHF I is rated higher (12 GHz), so a mixed U.FL / MHF-I mated pair is bounded by the lower ~6 GHz ceiling.

connectors.mating: Intermateable with U.FL / AMC / UMCC (same footprint). NOT compatible with MHF 4.
connectors.notes: I-PEX's U.FL-equivalent. MHF stands for 'micro high frequency' (I-PEX trademark). Footprint 3.0x3.1 mm, 2.0 mm male OD. I-PEX rates MHF I to 12 GHz on its own; mated with a ~6 GHz-rated U.FL, the pair is bounded by the U.FL ceiling (~6 GHz).

connectors.typical_wifi_use: Wi-Fi 6/6E/7 modules, mobile devices, M.2 cards
connectors.mating: Does NOT mate with U.FL or MHF I. A physically smaller family (also called W.FL / IPEX4).
connectors.notes: Smaller than U.FL/MHF1 (2.0x2.0 mm footprint, 1.5 mm male OD). The M.2 industry standard for modern compact modules.

connectors.mating: Cisco proprietary. Breaks out to RP-TNC, N-type or RP-N via Cisco adapter cables.
connectors.notes: Carries multiple RF chains through one connector: 4-port (2800/3800) or 8-port (9130AXE), plus digital pins that drive Self-Identifying Antennas (SIA) for auto-provisioning and EIRP control. Cisco does not publish the acronym; it is commonly cited as 'Digital Analog Radio Termination' (unverified by Cisco).

connectors.typical_wifi_use: AP enclosures, antenna mounting, panel pass-throughs
connectors.mating: Same mating rules as RP-SMA; mates only with RP-SMA.
connectors.notes: A common panel-mount variant of RP-SMA, not a distinct connector family. Used where a connector passes through an enclosure wall.

connectors.typical_wifi_use: DAS and cellular systems; rare in Wi-Fi
connectors.notes: Name comes from the 4.35 mm inner-conductor and 10 mm outer-conductor diameters (IEC 61169-54). Designed to replace 7/16 DIN in cellular with better PIM and size.

connectors.typical_wifi_use: High-power RF and DAS; typically cellular, not Wi-Fi
connectors.notes: Name from 16 mm outer-conductor and 7 mm center-conductor diameters. Robust, high power, good PIM. Large and heavy; being displaced by 4.3-10.

connectors.typical_wifi_use: Test equipment; not typically used for Wi-Fi antennas
connectors.impedance: 50 ohm (75 ohm version common in video; not for Wi-Fi)
connectors.mating: Mates only with BNC. 50 ohm and 75 ohm BNC look alike but should not be mixed.
connectors.notes: Quick bayonet coupling. Common on lab/test gear, rare on Wi-Fi antennas.

connectors.typical_wifi_use: None. This is a 75 ohm TV/CATV connector, not a Wi-Fi connector
connectors.notes: 75 ohm CATV/TV standard. The 75 ohm vs 50 ohm impedance mismatch causes significant loss on Wi-Fi gear. Do not use for WLAN. Folded in here from the former RF Connectors reference so the merge loses nothing.

vendor_trends.common_connector: RP-TNC; DART (Smart Antenna Connector) on 9130AXE and 2800/3800
Mostly internal antennas; RP-SMA on external-antenna models (e.g., E510)
RP-SMA on indoor external-antenna APs; N-type on outdoor (e.g., AP460e)
RP-SMA on connectorized radios (Force 400C/4600); N-type on some sector/dish antennas

size_order_note: Practical Wi-Fi-relevant ladder. Among the cellular trio, the 7/16 DIN body is physically the largest; this list ranks N first because it is the connector a Wi-Fi pro actually handles.

troubleshooting_class_top_6.intro: The connectors your students are most likely to encounter:

connectors.context: some embedded radios and IoT devices

troubleshooting_class_top_6.coverage_note: These six cover the large majority of what most Wi-Fi engineers meet in the field (field estimate, not a measured statistic).
